title: Industry urges Albanese government to relent on its ban on SMSF loans
full_text:
The Albanese government is under pressure from property industry players to partially reverse its unexpected decision to ban new self-managed super fund loans for residential property.
The forthcoming ban is a blanket one, encompassing new and existing homes – putting it at odds with the negative gearing changes announced in May’s federal budget where new homes are exempt.
Industry figures hope the impending rule change could be partially walked back, in a similar fashion to the way in which the minimum tax rates placed on trusts were later amended to exclude testamentary trusts. This had previously captured inheritances, left via a will, and critics called it a “death tax”.
As with the so-called death tax, experts are saying the residential loan ban for SMSFs could have far-reaching and unintended consequences, chiefly for the pipeline of new homes.
Ray White chief economist Nerida Conisbee said the SMSF loan ban would hamper the government’s densification efforts and the types of homes it was trying to build under the National Housing Accord.
“SMSF buyers are not spread evenly across the market. They are more likely to appear in specific types of stock: new apartments, townhouses, house-and-land packages and investor-oriented dwellings at lower price points,” Ms Conisbee said. “These are also the projects where pre-sales can be hardest to achieve when investor demand is weak.” Typically, for a new development to draw down on finance and start construction, some 60 per cent of units in a complex need to be pre-committed, with SMSF buyers commonly in the pool of those buying off the plan.
This means if one off-the-plan unit backed by an SMSF falls away, it could have a domino effect on the viability of the whole project. “If one buyer group is removed from that early stage, the impact is not limited to the individual purchase,” Ms Conisbee said. “It can affect whether the entire project starts, how many dwellings are delivered and how quickly new rental supply reaches the market.” The government says the rule change is small in scope, with fewer than 4000 SMSF loans written a year.
Developers estimate 30 per cent of off-the-plan purchases are backed by SMSFs. If the conservative 4000 figure is correct, then upwards of 13,000 units are at risk, though it’s unclear on the split between loans for new versus existing dwellings, or the proportion of off-the-plan purchasers that are leveraged.
Many in the sector say 4000 is likely a significant undershot of the true figure.
REA Group economist Luc Redman also said the ban could have a surprise effect where some SMSFs with enough cash look for established properties rather than take a punt on higher-risk off-the-plan builds as they are using more of their own capital.
“These changes won’t directly impact SMSFs’ desire to gain exposure to residential property as an asset class, but will most likely mean that exposure is skewed towards established dwellings rather than higher-density developments, exactly where supply is required.” Luke Hayes, Colliers’ director of residential project marketing, said market jitters for developers were already evident. “For developers … it can hurt the delivery of projects. There’s chat like ‘Wow I wasn’t expecting that’ … there’ll be a few disgruntled stakeholders for sure,” he said.
Oscar Stanley, Urban Development Institute of Australia president, said the rule change was at odds with the government’s home building goals. “A more sensible approach would be to actually encourage SMSF investment in new housing supply,” he said.
“That would align retirement savings with the national goal of increasing housing supply, support construction activity and help deliver more homes where they are most needed.” This proposal was backed by WITH, a non-bank lender and prominent provider of SMSF loans, in a research paper released earlier this week.
ABS figures for May show another slump in dwelling approvals as government approaches two years into its five-year timeline to build 1.2 million homes.
Under this target, 240,000 homes need to be built each year; Master Builders Australia says at an annualised rate, the government is 91,000 dwellings behind the eight-ball.
MBA chief economist Shane Garrett said the mid-2010s were the halcyon days for construction. “Then, we had a favourable mix of settings involving low interest rates, solid productivity, economic stability and an openness to foreign investment,” he said.
Brodie Haupt, CEO and co-founder of WITH, said the favourable conditions ended when APRA adjusted rules that limited investment loans to a certain percentage of a bank’s loan book.
Under the rules, banks were required to keep investment loan books growing at no greater than 10 per cent a year, and the proportion of interest-only loans was capped at 30 per cent of all new residential home loans. The rules were unwound at the end of 2018. Harrison Astbury is commercial property editor at REA Group.
